metadata.working_title: Modeling Decision-Making Under Uncertainty with Qualitative Outcomes

abstract_sketch.problem: Modeling decision-making under uncertainty typically relies on quantitative outcomes, but many real-world decisions (e.g., medical choices) are qualitative, posing problems for traditional utility-based models.
abstract_sketch.gap: Existing approaches either quantify an aspect of the outcome or treat categorical outcomes as equidistant, abandoning the complex qualitative nature of the choice.
abstract_sketch.approach: We introduce an Estimated Value hierarchical Bayesian model that assigns individualized subjective values to ordinal qualitative outcomes and fit it to choices in both monetary (quantitative) and medical (qualitative) domains across an in-person sample (N=66) and an online replication sample (N=332).
abstract_sketch.key_results: The Estimated Value model outperformed a classical utility model in the monetary domain and a no-subjective-parameters baseline in both domains. Ambiguity aversion (beta) in the medical domain was strongly associated with ambiguity aversion in the monetary domain (in-person mean slope = 0.76, 89% HDPi [0.63, 0.91]; online mean slope = 0.37, 89% HDPi [0.29, 0.44]).
abstract_sketch.impact: Extracting quantitative subjective values from qualitative outcomes enables characterization of individual uncertainty attitudes across a wider range of decisions and creates opportunities for parametric neuroimaging and psychiatric research in domains previously inaccessible to computational models.

sections.title: Introduction
content_bullets: Life is a series of decisions under uncertainty, ranging from quantitative (lotteries) to qualitative (choosing a restaurant, medical treatment).
Uncertainty decomposes into risk (known probabilities) and ambiguity (partially unknown probabilities); both typically elicit aversion in gains and vary across individuals.
Most computational studies of risk and ambiguity attitudes have used monetary outcomes; the few that studied other domains still quantified outcomes (M&Ms, months of lifespan, mg medication).
There is no straightforward computational framework for qualitative decisions, limiting neuroimaging and psychiatric applications.

We estimate risk and ambiguity attitudes in quantitative (monetary) and qualitative (medical) modalities using hierarchical Bayesian modeling to extract subjective values from choices with ordinal qualitative outcomes.
Objectives: (1) estimate subjective values for qualitative outcomes; (2) validate the model using monetary outcomes as a benchmark; (3) test whether ambiguity attitudes are consistent across domains.

subsections.title: Extracting Quantitative Values from Qualitative Outcomes
content_bullets: Context: Medical outcomes (slight / moderate / major / complete recovery) lack a common numeric scale.
Approach: Apply the Estimated Value model (equation 4) to medical decision data and compare against a No-Subjective-Parameters baseline (equation 5) using LOO cross-validation.
Finding: The Estimated Value model showed superior fit in both samples.
In-person sample added values: slight improvement 6.93 (SD 1.79); +9.00 (SD 2.62) for moderate; +7.04 (SD 2.89) for major; +4.18 (SD 2.41) for complete recovery.
Online sample added values: slight improvement 8.63 (SD 2.54); +12.62 (SD 4.25) for moderate; +4.66 (SD 2.56) for significant; +2.37 (SD 1.61) for complete recovery.
Interpretation: The model extracts individualized subjective values without assuming equal spacing between categories.

subsections.title: Model Validation and Comparative Analysis
content_bullets: Context: Validate the Estimated Value model against classical utility on data with known quantitative outcomes.
Approach: Fit four models to monetary decision data: Estimated Value, No-Subjective-Parameters, Classical Utility, and Classical Utility with Trembling-Hand choice function.
Finding: Estimated Value model had highest LOO in both samples.
In-person monetary added values: $5 = 7.22 (SD 1.60); +4.13 (SD 1.47) for $8; +6.23 (SD 2.18) for $12; +8.77 (SD 3.41) for $25.
Online monetary added values: $5 = 10.91 (SD 2.72); +4.13 (SD 1.84) for $8; +5.04 (SD 2.35) for $12; +3.92 (SD 2.18) for $25.
Interpretation: Extra degrees of freedom adapt to individual curvature; LOO guards against overfitting.

subsections.title: Cross-Domain Association Between Uncertainty Attitudes
content_bullets: Context: Are ambiguity attitudes a stable individual trait across qualitative and quantitative domains?
Approach: Robust regression of medical beta on monetary beta for each sample.
Finding: Strong positive association in-person (mean slope = 0.76, 89% HDPi [0.63, 0.91]); replicated online (mean slope = 0.37, 89% HDPi [0.29, 0.44]).

Interpretation: Ambiguity aversion generalizes across domains, supporting its use as a dispositional individual-difference measure; risk attitudes cannot be directly compared because they are absorbed into the estimated values.

subsections.title: Simulations
content_bullets: Context: A more flexible model could win through overfitting.

Approach: Simulate hypothetical agents choosing via the Classical Utility model with noise on alpha/beta parameters across (N, noise) pairs: (30, 0.1), (30, 0.3), (30, 0.5), (60, 0.3), (60, 0.5), (120, 0.5), (300, 0.5).
Finding: At low noise (0.1) the Classical Utility model wins; at higher noise (0.3, 0.5) the Estimated Value model has higher LOO and weight regardless of sample size.
Interpretation: The extra flexibility pays off when real participants deviate from a single fixed utility curve, as expected in empirical data.

sections.title: Discussion
content_bullets: Summary of contribution: a computational model that assigns quantitative values to qualitative outcomes and characterizes uncertainty attitudes across domains.
Qualitative outcomes challenge traditional risk/ambiguity modeling; prior work either quantified an outcome feature or treated categories as equidistant.
Clinical relevance: medical decisions are qualitatively described but often forced into quantitative surrogates (life-years, mg). The Estimated Value model preserves the original qualitative framing.
Why we focus on ambiguity rather than risk: risk is absorbed into the estimated values; ambiguity modulates choices within a domain and is directly comparable across domains.
Monetary validation: our model outperforms a classical utility function despite its extra flexibility, and simulations plus LOO cross-validation argue against overfitting.
Connection to framing effects and range-frequency theory: per-outcome curvature allows the model to accommodate context-dependent valuation.
Limitations: estimated values are on a relative scale without absolute units; risk cannot be compared directly across domains.
Future directions: use estimated values as parametric regressors in neuroimaging; extend to clinical populations and other qualitative domains.

subsections.title: Participants
content_bullets: Study 1 (in-person): 101 screened; after exclusions (incomplete n=10, MoCA < 26), final cognitively-healthy sample n=71 (32 female; age 18-88, mean 49.68 SD 22.3); after task-based exclusions (attention checks n=3, insufficient lottery choices n=2), final analysis sample n=66.
Study 2 (online): 404 recruited via mTurk; after dropouts (n=72), 332 analyzed (212 female; age 20-80, mean 49.362 SD 14.849).

subsections.title: Decision-Making Task
content_bullets: Choice between a certain gain ($5 / slight improvement) and a risky or ambiguous alternative with four possible outcomes.
Risk probabilities: 25%, 50%, 75%. Ambiguity occluder sizes: 24%, 50%, 74%. Monetary outcomes: $5, $8, $12, $25. Medical outcomes: slight / moderate / major / complete recovery.
Online study added a 100% ambiguity condition; lotteries presented twice vs. four times in-person.

subsections.title: Modeling Approach
content_bullets: Hierarchical Bayesian models with partial pooling; Classical Utility (eq 1), Trembling-Hand logistic (eq 3), Estimated Value (eq 4), No-Subjective-Parameters baseline (eq 5).
Risk attitude alpha, ambiguity attitude beta, inverse temperature gamma; same priors across samples; hyperpriors from prior data (mean alpha 0.72, mean beta 0.65).
Inference: PyMC 4.1.7 NUTS, 1000 draws, 1000 tune, 80% acceptance; convergence rHat < 1.01, effective sample size > 1000. ArviZ 0.17.1 used for LOO.

subsections.title: Simulations
content_bullets: Agents choose via Classical Utility with Gaussian noise on alpha and beta (alpha in [0.1, 1.6], beta in [-1.4, 1.4]).
Compared LOO and LOO weights for Estimated Value vs. Classical Utility.
